imposing, and rather tall, red-brown wall of sandstone. Climbing even higher to
the west, the cliff bears countless small cracks and fissures; many of these
could offer good hand and footholds to a climber, others might even hint at the
presence of some hidden fissure or cavern. Tough gorse bushes march along the
foot of the scarp.
name: Foot of a Cliff

desc:
Low, thorny shrubs are scattered amid a field of broken rock slabs and clumps of
coarse, straw-coloured grass. Some of the largest and flattest of the rocks are
concentrated so as to trace a barely discernible line, west and northeast across
the land, hinting that perhaps, at some time long ago, there might have been a
rough trail here.
name: Rock-strewn Plain

desc:
Brush and boulder compete with heather and grass to dominate the landscape, but
ultimately it is the yellow-green tussocks that hold sway, the coarse tufts
sprouting out and clinging to life all around. Rough accumulations of red-brown
rock pockmark the terrain, and a dense collection of them has created an arc
across the land, from south-west to east.
name: Rocky Tract

desc:
The land for miles around is blanketed by a patchwork quilt of purple heather
and tufts of yellow-green grass. Here and there a low patch of brambles stands
proud above the ground cover, stubbornly clinging to life in this bleak and
windswept landscape. The vista is hardly inviting, with no sign of any nearby
shelter, or even clean running water. The only possible refuge from the weather
would be by seeking shelter in the lea of a plateau to the south, but a bare
rock face would surely provide scant comfort.
name: Open Ground

desc:
A lofty plateau towers high above, and its sheer cliff face forms a wall that
sweeps to the northeast, and stretches away to the west. The sandstone bears
innumerable cracks and fissures, and in several places mighty slabs of the rock
appear close to breaking away entirely. A number of massive plates of the same
red-brown sandstone lie amid the bushes that surround the foot of the cliff, as
if to support to the idea that this is not a rock face that should be climbed.
name: Shadow of a High Mesa

desc:
Mighty slabs of reddish-brown rock lie scattered amongst the grass and bushes
here. To the west, the grass and bushes become more dense, with large boulders
sitting between the patches of tangled brush. More earth and rock can be noticed
to the south, where slabs have been piled up slightly, forming a narrow pass to
an area that is more open. The land appears to incline slightly towards the
east.
name: Tall Plateau

desc:
As a line of cliffs turns away to the south, the band of prickly furze that
stands along its foot stretches westward. As brush and rock wall diverge, the
vegetation becomes more scattered, giving way to more of the coarse grasses that
characterise these lands. Drawing south, the ground also becomes markedly
rockier, with boulders of ever increasing size scattered amongst the bushes.
name: Expanse of Brush and Boulder

desc:
This land is not the meadow that might have been expected standing leeward of
the plateau. Perhaps that it lies in shadow so much of the time can account for
the insipid colour of the grass and of the bushes that line the base of the
cliffs. But the soil should be richer and deeper in a spot so sheltered from the
prevailing winds, more suited to lush vegetation. Instead, a few thin patches of
grass grow between rugged mounds of rock, and everywhere else lies nothing but a
bed of smaller stones and gravel. A structure was constructed to the south,
behind the natural rock and earthen-walls that form here, secured by a sturdy
gate.
name: Scarred Land

desc:
A vast table of rock is here, with piles of slabs in the northern corners, which
appears to form a narrow pass to the northern plains. The plateau curves to the
southwest of here, and not too far into the distance it comes to an end in a
high, rocky promontory that must afford a commanding view of all of the
surrounding lands. The red-brown stone underfoot bares only the hardiest of
plant life, and nearing the edge of the mesa the ground underfoot becomes quite
unstable.
name: Edge of a Mesa

desc:
Sloping almost imperceptibly upward to the west, the plateau here stands high
above the surrounding plains. The red-brown sandstone is covered by only the
barest trace of topsoil, buffeted as it is by the harsh prevailing winds. And so
only the hardiest of vegetation can cling to life here, with tough roots that
force their way down into minute cracks in the rock. The rim of the mesa is
particularly cracked, and appears to be quite unstable.
name: Windswept Mesa

desc:
The flat table top that defines the plateau slopes gently and leads upward to
the west, so that by this point it rises to a significant height above the
surrounding lands both north and south. A river flows along the southern foot of
the plateau. The waters look quite deep at this point, so that it should be
fairly safe to make the jump down, but the sheer, moss-covered rock-face would
probably prevent any ascent back to this area.
name: Plateau Overlooking a River

desc:
The land surrounding this plateau presents a bleak and windswept aspect. Little
vegetation can thrive on this stark and rocky plateau. High above the
surrounding plains, the land here is buffeted by the very harshest of the wind,
rain and snow. As a result, the topsoil is thin and only a few small, hardy,
pine-looking plants cling to life among the rocks.
name: Plateau above the Plains

desc:
The plateau stretches out far to the east and south. To the west and north it
comes to an abrupt end at the top of a cliff. The rocky precipice looks quite
unstable, but some places appear less fragile than others. Red-brown rocks and
small boulders lie littered across the ground, and little plant life clings to
life in this bleak and windswept location.
name: Windy Bluff

desc:
Red-brown rocks and a few small boulders lie scattered across the ground with
virtually no vegetation to be seen. The land here atop the plateau is punished
by all that the weather can throw at it, and only bare rock remains, making it
impossible for all but the hardiest of pine-like plants to cling to life.
name: Rocky Table

desc:
A bleak, windswept countenance characterises the land here atop the plateau.
Loose rocks and small boulders lie scattered all about, but there is little
plant life to be seen. Instead of lush green grass, or even the coarse yellow
tussocks that can be seen on the plain below, the ground here is the same red-
brown colour of the rocks.
name: Edge of the Plateau

desc:
The land rises gently to the west and south, climbing toward the top of a flat
and windswept table of ground. Thick tussocks of coarse, yellow grass cling to
life like a stubbly beard on the stony slope from here to the plains below. A
few tiny patches of low weeds sprout up amid this growth, but no taller plant
life is to be seen.
name: Escarpment

desc:
Climbing gently to the west and falling away to the east, this tongue of land
marks the end of a long, flat plateau that stretches into the distance to the
west. Red-brown rocks lie scattered amid the coarse grass and thin weeds that
cover the ground. But no bush or shrub can cling to life on this bleak and
windswept slope.
name: Rising Land

desc:
Medium-sized pine trees line a rim of this corner near the road along the north
and east side of the glade. An old structure can be noticed between the pines to
the north, and large boulders stand between them to the east. Just west of here,
an ancient and long-since-abandoned road begins its gradual ascent toward a flat
table of higher ground, the pathway having nearly vanished from view. The land
falls away toward a river, snaking through the country to the south, as it
climbs slightly toward the mouth of a long vale to the east.
name: Glade of Pine Trees

desc:
The land slopes gently here, rising in the north to well above the height of the
river. Dipping down almost to the level of the water to the south, much of the
ground is quite muddy. A thick carpet of long grass grows all about, but toward
the water's edge it is forced to compete with the sedges that flourish in the
wetter ground.
name: Gentle Grade

desc:
The river flows from east to south, gathering momentum as it travels onward
through its narrow, rocky channel. The bank to the north looms steep and tall
above the water, the cold stone slick with moss. However, though the western
bank is equally hard and rocky, withstanding the efforts of the roiling waters
to push their way relentlessly forward, it nevertheless stands at a lesser
height above the surface of the river.
name: Small Rushing River

desc:
The waters of a small river are funnelled through a fairly narrow valley here.
The vale is hemmed in both north and south by tall, hilly ridges, and at the
valley floor only a rather thin, rocky channel remains for the waterway, causing
it to dash and splash as it makes its way westward on towards the distant
Bruinen.
name: A Small Rushing River

desc:
Tumbling along a narrow, rocky channel the small river rushes on through the
narrowing valley. Dashing against rocks and boulders, both on the streambed and
along its shores, the waters roil about, casting a fine, cool mist into the air
throughout the vale.
name: A Small Rushing River

desc:
This small but lively river runs from east to west through the valley, making
its way inexorably down toward its eventual meeting with the mighty Bruinen. To
the west its channel steepens and its course becomes narrower, forcing the
waters to rush ever faster on their way, dashing against rocks, and creating a
fine mist which hangs like a haze over the whole valley.
name: Fast-flowing River

desc:
East and north, the ground climbs gently toward a flat table of land that looms
above the plains. To the south a small, but lively river cuts its channel along
the foot of the plateau where the ground suddenly drops. Between the deep fall
and large boulders at the edge of the plateau here to the river below, passage
to the south would be impossible from here. Thick patches of vibrant purple
heather compete with tussocks of coarse, yellow grass, and this blanket of
vegetation hides the red-brown rock beneath it almost completely.
name: Sloping Ground near a Cliff

desc:
Sloping steeply upward to the north and west, the ground climbs towards the top
of a large table of land that looms above the plains. To the south the slope
falls away, approaching a merry little river that cuts along the foot of the
plateau. Between the drop and cliff wall, it is impossible to reach the river
below safely from here. Grey rocks and stones tumble amid the coarse tussocks of
yellow grass. Scattered patches of purple heather soften the rocky ground.
name: Sloping Ground near a Cliff

desc:
A flat table of land stretches out far to the north, east, and west. To the
south, a small river travels along the foot of the plateau. Sloping gently
downward to the east, the plateau here stands high above the river. The ground
above is peppered with loose rocks, and there is virtually no vegetation of any
type to be seen.
name: Atop a Low Cliff

desc:
A small river snakes along the foot of the plateau below. The ground slopes
gently upward to the west, as the flat table of land rises above the plain, and
even here it stands quite tall above the surrounding lands. A few thin tussocks
of coarse, yellow grass nestle amid the tumbled rocks and stones, but no other
vegetation is to be seen. The cliff edge is ragged and uneven, and it seems that
standing too close might cause some of the stone to break away.
name: Rugged Plateau

desc:
A vast, flat table of land stands high above the plains and stretches off to the
east. Reaching its highest point here, it also comes to an abrupt end. Loose,
crumbling, red-brown rock defines the margin of the plateau, and little
vegetation clings to life on this windswept land. At the westernmost edge of the
mesa a massive boulder rests on the ground. The sounds of rippling water can be
heard below where a brimming waterfall is noticed.
name: Rocky Promontory

desc:
Rising quite steeply to the west, the land gains the top of a plateau. The
fragmented remains of an ancient road sweep past this expanse of land, defining
a border to both west and north. A third boundary is marked by the waters of a
lively little river whose waters have apparently breached the bank on many
occasions, to leave the margins of this land almost swampy. There it is coarse
sedge that flourishes. But in the rest of this area thick grass predominates.
name: Muddy Riverbank

desc:
Some thorny bushes are scattered here and there in these grasslands, but not
much else grows here. The grass itself is patchy and not very dense. There is
little to obstruct the passage of the wind as it blows through the land.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
A long stretch of sharp-bladed grass cuts its way through the plains here
flanked by patches of dirt. The ground is quite uneven with small mounds rising
and dipping into tiny ditches all around. Some rocky hills can be seen to the
north but the horizon is dominated by the Misty Mountains to the east.
name: Grasslands

desc:
The hills here are quite dense with no even footing to be had. Boulders, rocks,
and pebbles cover the slopes with the occasional patch of grass or lonely bush.
The land to the east and west is steep and covered in loose scree but easy
passage can be seen traversing to the north or south.
name: Rocky Route through the Hills

desc:
A small but lively river winds along the northern flank of the ridge. The slope
down to the water is so steep that only a slippery coating of dark green moss
clings to life on the rocky face. The southern slope is less severe, but still
quite steep, and peppered with so many loose rocks and boulders that a descent,
though possible, should only be undertaken with great care. Both east and west
of here, the land climbs up out of this rocky saddle.
name: Along a Narrow Ridge

desc:
The rocky ridge comes to an abrupt end here. Its northern and western flanks are
sheer, falling away to a river that skirts along its feet, far below. A coating
of slippery, dark green moss is all that clings to those rocky walls. To the
south the slope is gentler, yet the innumerable rocks and small boulders that
are scattered across its surface would make descent to the plain beyond at least
a little risky.
name: End of a Ridge

desc:
Tailing off to the west, the hills rise sharply eastward, and within a very
short distance the ground ahead is almost bare of vegetation. To the south the
slope stands steeper still, blocking travel in that direction, but to the north
the land makes a more gentle descent towards the bottom of a narrow valley.
name: Climbing into the Hills

desc:
The land rises gently to the east as it climbs along the length of the vale. To
the south it slopes more steeply, as the valley floor gives way to the flanks of
a tall range of hills. A lively river rushes through the narrowing glen, its
waters leaving a fine mist behind them.
name: A Misty Valley

desc:
The only way to climb these hills is to scramble from rock to rock. Massive
boulders and loose scree are scattered everywhere, and very little vegetation
has managed to take hold in the thin soil. The land rises sharply to the east
and falls away more gradually to the west, as it descends toward the plains
below. The terrain to the south rises sharply into some hills.
name: Rocky Route through the Hills

desc:
A line of pine trees along the western side of the glade rise next to the walls
of an old structure. The structure is hard to discern from here between the pine
trees and large boulders. The wood appears aged, as if the building has been
there for quite a long time. To the south the medium-sized pines grow more thick
toward the east, blocking any travel from here to the south directly. The pine
trees curve around the north side of the glade and continue east. To the east
there are weeds and patches of coarse grass.
name: Glade behind a Ruined Inn

desc:
The land here is bare in the centre and surrounded by a thick line of silvery
pines, smaller trees, and thorny shrubs along the west, south and east. Some
boulders are also scattered along the tree line, large and all differ in shape
from one another, which also prevents any travel in either of those three
directions. The sound of water can be heard somewhere to the west or south.
name: Glade of Pine Trees

desc:
As the river flows from the north back to a westerly path, its surging waters
undercut the southerly bank, creating a small rocky prominence that looms above
the channel. The banks stand sheer above the waterway, their faces covered in
slippery moss, making ascent impossible, but here and there wide cracks can be
seen in the rock face, as if parts of the wall were loose.
name: Small Rushing River

desc:
The channel starts to widen here, and as it does so the waters of the river
begin to lose momentum. To the south a tall hilly ridge looms above the river,
and its sheer, stony face sternly blocks the water. To the north, the bank
descends almost to the level of the water. It also begins to yield a little,
allowing the river to occasionally forge a breach as if seeking a path back to
the north.
name: Widening Channel

desc:
As the river flows from the east, it strives to cut a pathway forward through
the rocky bank ahead, but the cold, wet stone stubbornly stands against the
writhing waters, forcing them to turn aside. They burgeon to the north, where
the bank seems softer, making the ground beyond quite swampy. But in the end the
path of least resistance is to the south.
name: Turning Channel

desc:
A lively little river flows from the north, steadily losing momentum, as it
enters an ever wider channel. To the south, where the foot of a craggy ridge of
hills slopes down to meet the plains, a cold, unyielding, rocky shore forces the
waters to turn westward, where they approach an old, decrepit roadway.
name: Nearing a Ford

desc:
Travelling at the bottom of a rising plain to the north, and hemmed in to the
south by the footings of an ancient roadway, the river is funnelled into a
narrower, but deeper channel. As the waters tumble down from the raised river
bed of the ford that lies just east of here, they swirl about, finding their way
in this deeper course. Though not yet too much deeper than the ford, the river
is nevertheless just a bit too deep and a little too lively to permit wading.
name: On a Shallow River

desc:
The bank to the north grows taller and steeper as the river travels westward
along the foot of the plateau. An ancient roadway, by now in a very poor state
of repair, journeys beside the river here, and its stony footings form a firm
bank that will not permit the channel to grow wider. In this confined course,
the waters continue to flow along at a lively pace, but further westward the
channel begins to widen.
name: Along the River

desc:
East and west, the river telescopes into the distance. Its northern bank here is
tall, steep and rocky, climbing to the top of a large plateau. But to the south
the bank is softer and lower, and the waters have taken advantage of this to cut
a wider channel through which they can flow lazily along.
name: On the Lazy Waters

desc:
A tall cliff looms above the surface of the river as it journeys westward along
the foot of a tall plateau. The rock wall is almost vertical, but pitted with
numerous small fissures, suggesting that an ascent might be possible. To the
south a lower bank borders the waters, and a faint trail can be seen travelling
beside the watercourse.
name: River by the Foot of a Plateau

desc:
Sheltered from northerly winds by a plateau which looms above it to the north,
the river flows lazily westward. Its waters here are quite deep, and a pair of
heavy wooden pilings disappear into its murky depths a short distance from the
southern bank. A few broken and rotten shards of wood bob gently, tugged by the
current, but held weakly to the pilings by rusty, square-headed nails.
name: Deep and Lazy Stretch of Water

desc:
Turning south, the water brims here against the western cliff wall and plateau
to the north, before it spills over more cliffs that form a waterfall. Green
vines scale the cliff and disappear beyond to the north, and the cliff wall
appears to continue far to the east along the river. The water here is deep and
clear, with tiny bubbles that keep bursting at the surface.
name: Brimming Waterfall

desc:
The sheer rock wall has tapered outwards as it gained elevation, such that a
narrow ledge perches here, about three quarters of the way to the cliff top.
Buffeted by the elements, this slender shelf would seem a precarious and most
inhospitable location. Yet even here where no visible soil remains a tiny
thicket of stunted bushes clings to life.
name: Rocky Shelf

desc:
The roar of a waterfall to the north drowns out many sounds. Boulders and swift
currents dominate this area. To the impassable north, water plummets down into
the boulder-strewn riverbed. Looking east, a trail is visible. To the west lies
a swampy region, while a look south reveals a grassy plain.
name: Boulder Choked Riverbed

desc:
The deep river flows by a small landing, where boats can be left. The landing is
constructed from oaken poles that are connected to one another by strong ropes.
The landing has several extra ropes that can be used to secure boats. A little
hovel just next to the landing has an entrance on the south side. The river's
westward flow continues as it passes the sloping fields on its north bank.
name: Small Landing

desc:
The hovel is smaller inside than it looked from the outside. The majority of the
tools that are strewn about the floor and walls were used for fieldwork and
fishing. The western wall's modest window offers a clear glimpse of the big
river as it rushes down its course. The remaining space is taken up by benches
and shelving.
name: Smuggler's Hovel

desc:
This small trail consists mostly of dry grasses and an occasional stone or weed.
However to the north the grass becomes greener as it grows near the banks of a
slow-moving river. An old, but useable landing provides a way for boats to cross
the river just below a waterfall to the west. In the distance to the east an
ancient, broken road makes its way north and south.
name: Trail off a Road

desc:
Standing some ten feet above even the highest part of the plateau, this place
affords a breathtaking view of the lands all around. Over fifteen feet across,
this monstrous slab of rock has been scoured flat by the elements. Tiny cracks
and fissures, the scars of countless cycles of frost and thaws, pit the ruddy
surface. But not a single plant, even to the smallest poppy or tiniest clump of
stonecrop, can be seen anywhere. Not a single patch of phlox clings to life
here.
name: Colossal, Flat Boulder

desc:
The trail extends to the east and west, bordered to the north by a lazy river.
To the south, in the middle of this otherwise barren landscape, lies a small
forest, probably gaining its strength from underground streams seeking their own
paths away from the river. Past the water, a steep plateau can be seen in the
distance. Green, healthy grass grows near the banks of the water.
name: Trail off a Road

desc:
Markings of a seldom used trail start here and head off towards the west. Bare
patches of earth interrupt the otherwise solid growth of dry grass, which grows
in contrast to the greener grass on the banks of the river in the north. The dry
grass and occasional stone of an ancient, broken road lie eastwards.
name: Trail off a Road

desc:
The murky water bubbles and foams as it flows down the widening river at a
steady pace from a narrow channel in the east. Some rapids that can be heard
from further upstream that cause the surface of the water to be covered in white
foam. Not much can be seen below the surface of the water.
name: Muddy Waters

desc:
The rivulet flows from east to west only to be interrupted by a grey boulder in
its centre. The boulder protrudes out from the murky water, itself heavily
clouded with brown silt, and causes the current to foam and splash. A few sticks
and remains of dead plants drift along in the water.
name: Murky Rivulet

desc:
Run-off and rain water from the lakes and slopes of the Misty Mountains have
trickled down and accumulated into a pool here, where the land has been eroded
into a slight depression. To the west the ground juts out and forms a small hill
and a bank for the pool. As the water streams in, it is released once again to
the north, as a small stream. The water is dirty and soiled from the sediment
that has washed down from the hills and mountains to the east.
name: Muddy Pool

desc:
A slow-moving stream starts its way down the barren land here, a result of the
overflow of water running down the slopes of the towering Misty Mountains to the
east. The water forms a pool in the south where the land is higher, then slowly
gains speed as gravity forces it down into the grassy plains to the west.
Foothills have formed to the south, dotted here and there with sparse foliage.
name: Bend in a Muddy Stream

desc:
Though a few stumps and rare patches of dirt are visible, most of the land here
is covered in a thick film of soft sod. Wind snakes along the tops of the blades
of grass making them dance and bow. A river can be seen to the north alongside a
muddy bank. The ground rises to some rocky hills to the south.
name: Grass Covered Plains

desc:
A large amount of sediment and debris have pooled here in a shallow area around
the stream creating a sort of marshy swamp. To the east and west can be heard
running water, while passage south is impossible because of piled branches and
detritus. Travel north leads to more of the ever present plains that dominate
the region.
name: Shallow Crossing in a Muddy Stream

desc:
The languid current of the muddy waters of this small stream gathers momentum
here as the incline of the land increases markedly to the southwest. The slope
of the lands to the west continues its downward journey, bottoming out into a
soggy plain. The amount of sediment and debris carried by this stream begins to
decrease, as evidenced by the continuously increasing clarity of the water.
name: Muddy Stream

desc:
The water follows the steep decline of the surrounding landscape, flowing
southward into the lower part of the valley. A tall hill rises up to the east,
blocking the wind which normally blows down from the Misty Mountains in the
distance. The land on either side of the stream is rendered inaccessible due to
steep cuts in the land.
name: Dip in a Stream

desc:
The water is shallow and clear as it turns toward the west and continues for
several miles. The surrounding land is soggy and overgrown with reeds and
rushes. The occasional fish can be seen darting through the water in search of
insects to nip. A steep bank of earth to the east and south makes travel
impossible in that direction.
name: Bend in a Stream

desc:
The stream continues its westward flow, bordered to the north by a vast stretch
of soggy marshland and to the south by a wide flat grassland. Reeds and other
plant life grows steadily, rising out of the water and creeping up the bank. The
sound of buzzing insects can be heard along the banks.
name: Small Stream

desc:
The stream flows steadily westward, gathering momentum as it travels through the
soggy valley. A slight wind can usually be felt blowing from the east, where one
can see the snow-covered peaks of the Misty Mountains far off in the distance.
The clear water of the stream is bordered on the north and south by mostly
barren plains. Steep, debris-choked banks prevent any travel north.
name: Small Stream

desc:
The water of a small stream turns its course southward here, following the lay
of the surrounding valley. The banks of the stream are soggy and covered in
reeds and mud making passage over them impossible. In the distance to the
northwest, a small grouping of tall trees has grown, seemingly out of place in
the otherwise featureless valley.
name: Bend in a Stream

desc:
The small stream from the northeast collides with the mountain stream to the
southeast here, creating an even larger body of water which continues its
journey westward. Where the water meets, small currents and eddies are formed,
churning the surface and creating a soft, white foam on. A wooden bridge has
been built nearby, allowing travellers to cross.
name: Joining of Two Streams

desc:
The current of the westward flowing river is intensified here, where the waters
of two smaller streams, having joined forces just a short distance to the east,
are channelled along their common course. The swift water boils and bubbles at
the surface, sending white eddies and ripples to and fro. Above you a wooden
bridge spans the river, blocking any view of the skies.
name: Under the Bridge

desc:
The water rushes by quickly, flowing west with a renewed strength from the
combining of the two smaller streams in the east. Foamy white caps of tiny waves
ripple along the top of the otherwise clear water, dancing to and fro. The river
banks rise up steeply here, hindering movement south.
name: Swift River

desc:
The river follows the contours of the land, flowing here from east to north in a
sweeping curve. The combining of the rivers to the east has increased the
current, making it harder to swim upstream. The southern and western banks rise
steeply and are covered with slick mud, making access to the shore in those
directions quite impossible.
name: Bend in a River

desc:
Evidence of mudslides can be seen on the bank of the water to the east, due to
its steep slope. The water flows by, gradually following the lay of the land
from the south to the west. An small drop in the land west of here forces the
water to tumble down some rocks, creating a tiny waterfall.
name: Bend in a River

desc:
The clear water of the river grows shallower here due to a build-up of rock and
sediment. A sudden break in the grass and thicket choked plains creates a tiny
waterfall, some four feet high. The south bank rises high enough to block any
passage in that direction.
name: Beneath a Waterfall

desc:
The shallow river flows from the east to the west. The surrounding fields are
covered with patches of dry, brown grass and some small thorn bushes grow on the
riverbanks. Several birds hover above the water, which is the home for many
small water animals and various fish, searching for easy prey.
name: Shallow River

desc:
Just southeast of where the Ancient Broken Road bends again on its long journey
between south and north, wide brushlands spread out before you. The lands lie
quite untouched and dense brush grows in abundance. Faint traces of old
campfires can be seen here and there, where patches of bare earth, edged by
yellowish grass, dot the landscape.
name: Brushy Field

desc:
Wide brushlands spread out between the stream to the east and the ancient road
in the west. The land slopes quite dramatically down to lower grounds in the
south and west where land and waterway are almost level, leaving the brush thick
by the rich supply of water. To the north a vertical wall, probably the result
of an age-old landslide, effectively prevents passage.
name: Brush Choked Plain

desc:
Here, the brush gives way to more of the largely featureless plains that
dominate the region. Grass rises from the red soil, mixed with the odd patch of
brush here and there. An ancient, broken, road runs just to the west, obediently
conforming to the meandering of the waterway. To the north are great thickets of
brush, and a steep, muddy bank to the south blocks passage to the river.
name: Fields near a River

desc:
Surrounded to the east and south by muddy waters, this area is constantly
covered by mud from regular flooding. So much so, that the dense mud makes
passage south impossible. To the north, the land slopes up towards the ancient
road some distance away. A lone hill rises just beyond the water to the east.
name: Wetlands

desc:
Sharp rocks and large boulders litter the red earth of this field, making
passage somewhat difficult. Easier paths for the ancient road and to the nearby
water exist, leaving this area desolate and largely forgotten save for by the
odd creature looking to stay out of common areas of travel.
name: Rocky Field

desc:
A small forest of trees grows here, providing a place hidden away from the sun
for such creatures who prefer the darkness over the light. A cooking pit filled
with scorched bones and charred bits of wood and flesh dominate the area. Logs
and boulders are placed around it for some sort of seating arrangement. The
skulls of unfortunate humanoids are randomly spiked about as some sort of savage
decoration.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
Surrounded by densely packed trees, this glade is impenetrable from all sides
except from the south. Although the close forest is very tight, the ground here
is trampled smooth by the feet of large humanoids. Trash and forest debris are
piled into crude nests for the use of some sort of large and apparently foul
smelling creatures. An acrid scent of urine and faeces competes with the smell
of rotting vegetation and flesh.
name: Protected Glade

desc:
A flat, almost featureless plain sprawls before the river. Vegetation is richer
due to the presence of water, but the soil still is red, and dusty in places
during the warmer months. The riverbank to the west drops sharply by several
feet, but to the south the ground draws more level with the water.
name: Plains by a River

desc:
Multiple giant feet have trampled the forest floor into a hard, compact mass
devoid of vegetation. This is one of the hunting areas of the murderous forest
trolls which infest forests across Arda, bringing evil and destruction wherever
they go. Broken trees and branches are strewn about, mingled with equally
shattered bones of animals and humanoids alike.
name: Dark Forest by a River

desc:
What could have been a beautiful, peaceful forest has been defiled by giant and
destructive creatures that seem to care only to satisfy their violent and
destructive urges. Trees bear scars from claws and teeth - mute testimony to the
violence that takes place here when trolls go hunting or let their rage and
stupidity overwhelm them in a frenzied orgy of violence.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
Red dust and rocks are scattered across the plains, making movement difficult.
The eastern forest casts a shadow in the area and blocks the wind and rain that
usually blows over this area. To the south, rushing water can be heard.
name: Plains by a River

desc:
Rough, green grass grows here in the rich soils alongside the river to the north
of here. Signs of various wildlife can be seen leading to and from the forests
east of the riverbank. The eastern trees rise high, blocking visibility in that
direction. To the south the plains head to the far side of the eastern forest. A
trail leads west into a field.
name: Grasslands by a River

desc:
The ground is damp in most places and soggy in some and fertile here. The river
to the south feeds the plain here with abundant hydration. To the north the
fields continue. To the east, the plains next to the river continue. The air
here teems with life from small animals, birds, and insects.
name: Plain by a River

desc:
Due to the encircling rivers, these lands are well nourished by the groundwater
and have grown lush and green. A few minor bushes occupy the landscape as well,
adding to the diet of the local fauna. The ground becomes moist to the south,
while close to the river to the north, a landing can be spotted.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
The majority of the vegetation in this desolate area is brown, dry grass, with a
few scattered small thickets of prickly brush. The fields continue in all
directions. Most of the land consists of clay and limestone, yet the shallow
river to the north has brought nutritious silt and water, making the grass to
the north, grow longer and greener. A road can be seen far to the south.
name: Endless Wasteland

desc:
The grassy land slopes abruptly from the brushy plains in the west towards the
water of the shallow river to the north and east. The river can be seen quite
clearly from this vantage point, as well as a small waterfall further upstream
to the east. The ground consists of dried grass and stony reddish brown soil.
name: Field near a River

desc:
Some fields here are composed of a dry, rough grass with some scattered thickets
of thorn bushes. A few chalk and limestone rocks are scattered throughout the
red soil. To the south, the land plunges into an impossible to climb descent,
which slowly turns into a valley. In the distant east, the peaks of the Misty
Mountains rise over the horizon.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Tangled brush blends in with the long brown-dry grass upon the red soil. On
three sides this barren land ends in a precipice. An ancient road crosses the
land south and east, while to the north you can hear a swift river on its way
westward. Vast grasslands spread out to the west.
name: Ridge Surveying the Land

desc:
The barren lands are covered with small patches of brown, dry grass and once
every thousand feet vegetated by a thin thicket of thorny bushes. A large road
lies to the south, steadily finding its way through the sloping landscape. The
way south is impassable due to a steep cliff which juts downward. Small stones
and boulders lie scattered on the ground, the only remnants of a river that once
flowed here.
name: Cliff near the Road

desc:
The tunnel travels from the west and bends sharply in the centre, leading north
to another section of the tunnel. The ceiling was reinforced here with wooden
beams and eight large timbers that were reinforced with metal to use as braces
from the ground to the ceiling. Four beams, two on each side to the west of the
bend, and another four beams stand to support the ceiling to the north. A thick
dusting covers the ground and lingers within the air, and several spider webs
are in the south-east corner at the sharp bend in the tunnel.
name: Bend in a Tunnel Underpass

desc:
The tunnel travels from a north and south direction, appearing to widen greatly
to the north where a thick darkness consumes whatever lies beyond there. The
ceiling was reinforced here with wooden beams and six large timbers that were
reinforced with metal to use as braces from the ground to the ceiling. Although
the wood does not appear aged, it is covered in a thick dust that would imply
this underpass has not been used frequently.
name: Tunnel Underpass

desc:
The profound cavity is dark and deep, with niches along the south portion to
help with footing over the earthen basin that was naturally formed here. Over
the basin to the south, from what is visible from here at least, the cavern or
tunnel continues. A draft is felt somewhere from above, but the darkness is so
thick there it would be impossible to guess from which direction. Small rocks
and pebbles are scattered along the centre of the cavity, with dust lingering
along the earth, debris and even in the air.
name: Dark Underground Cavity

desc:
This fairly narrow tunnel was apparently excavated by some form of wildlife, for
its walls bear what can only be claw marks. Turning slightly, so as to protect
what lies beyond from any onslaught by the elements, the tunnel slopes gently
downwards to the west, exploiting the natural rock strata to make digging
easier, and take advantage of the natural drainage they provide.
name: Narrow Tunnel

desc:
Having turned slightly as it cut deeper into the ground, the tunnel here is
quite protected from the elements, creating a small but cosy nook. But even so,
someone has taken great trouble to insulate the area with rolled heaps of
bedding, so thick that they could even smother the sound of a footfall. Dry
bundles of grass are mixed with leaves, twigs, and soft, twisted roots to line
the rocky floor of the tunnel.
name: Cosy Sett

desc:
Dry and dusty in the summer months, this field turns to a muddy mess when wet,
or a frozen wasteland in the winter. Thin grass and miserable brush rise from
the red soil, seeking what opportunity for growth might exit. To the north some
sort camp can be seen. The Ancient Broken road continues to the west. All other
directions give way to more of the same bleak plains.
name: Dry Field

desc:
The grass clad slopes roll downwards in soft, green waves from the hills in the
south and west. Between the hills and the mountains in the east, a narrow strip
of flat land stretches out, fed in large part by water from a pool that has
formed in a small depression just to the north. A couple of short trees and some
bushes grow together in a small clump on a slight rise, near to the body of
water.
name: Sloping Grasslands

desc:
Waves of thick grass cascade over the land enveloping the area in an ocean of
green. Small shrubs dot the landscape, while boulders add texture to the scenic
but windy view. Some rocky hills can be seen blocking passage to the south.
name: Grassy Fields

desc:
Except for the tall mountains rising up in the east, this small plateau is the
highest spot for many miles. The plateau is clad in short, green grass and
spotted by patches of bright flowers. To the west, the ground rolls downwards
into the river, albeit at an angle preventing passage in that direction. The
plateau becomes narrower to the north, eventually transforming into a ridge,
overlooking the stream that bends westward far below.
name: Higher Grounds

desc:
The land is higher here compared to the muddy lowlands below to the north, east
and west. A ridge has formed as a result of the erosion of the water sweeping
down from the incline of the Misty Mountains to the east. Rock and stone has
been embedded over the years into the ground here, forcing the incoming waters
to go around. To the south-east lie the rolling foothills of the Misty
Mountains. All around are scattered broken bones and the torn, smashed remains
of humanoid and animal corpses.
name: Ridge in the Plains

desc:
Tall strong grass grows on the flat grassland that spreads out widely, flowing
between the stream that cuts the land diagonally, and the mountains towering up
in the east. To the north another river runs its westward journey down from the
mountains and away through the vast wilderness. The grasslands rise quickly in
the east, where hills roll beneath the foot of the mountains.
name: At the Foot of a Hill

desc:
Two streams meet just west of here, one coming from the northeast and the other
from the southeast. Despite this, the land has avoided becoming marsh-like due
to it's slight elevation above the water, although it is still somewhat damp.
Further inland however, the ground grows somewhat more dry and the grass grows
taller. In the east, towards the Misty Mountains, some foothills rise out of the
grasslands.
name: Fields by a River

desc:
The land starts to rise here as it draws closer to the mountains. The stream
that runs to the north is now far below and the loose, sandy hillside that leads
down there is far too difficult to climb. Wide fields roll away to the south and
another waterway can be seen, running northwest from the Misty Mountains. To the
east, more hills rise up, taller and larger than this one.
name: On a Low Hill

desc:
From the top of this tall hill the view of the surrounding landscape is good.
Rolling grasslands spread out far into the south, between the stream that cuts
through the land and the natural barricade of the Misty Mountains in the east.
In the northeast another stream comes bending down from the mountains, crashing
into the southerly one not too far west of here. The ground rises slightly to
the north and slopes downwards again in the northwest.
name: High on a Hill

desc:
As a prelude to the towering mountains, these hills stand below the vast slopes
in the east, linking them together with the rolling grasslands in the west. The
rocky ground does not allow for much growth, and the water trickling down along
cracks and crevices continues down towards the fields, not being able to sink
into the bedrock. A small stream begins its westward journey far below to the
north, twisting and turning through the landscape.
name: Foothills of the Misty Mountains

desc:
The grass grows taller here, closer to the water, than in the eastern fields.
Small trails, trodden frequently by animals, run seemingly irregularly across
the land in intricate patterns, some suddenly stopping at holes in the ground.
To the north, the ground slopes slightly upwards to a low hill, the beginning of
the mountains in the east.
name: At the Side of a Hill

desc:
Hills rise up against the river to the west, with steep cliffs making passage in
that direction and the south impossible. To the north and east lie more rolling
plains. Even further east, the Misty Mountains rise majestically over the
region.
name: Hills by a River

desc:
The small river flowing southwest of here announces its presence with a soft
roaring as it picks up speed. As it has hollowed out the ground over the ages,
sinking deeper and deeper, the eastern bank has risen several feet above the
water. Thick brush stretch out in the southeast, towards the mountains.
name: Brushy Fields near a Stream

desc:
Vast fields of green grass surround you. Quite close to the north, a steep
hillside rises up from the otherwise flat lands around. A thin, lonely tree
stands in the middle of the field, surrounded by some short bushes, and far in
the east, the slopes of the Misty Mountains start their upward journey towards
the sky.
name: Fields below the Hills

desc:
As the hard, rocky ground in the east loses its dominance to more nourishing
earth, grass and small plants are able to grow more freely. The fields start off
hesitantly, but quickly gain confidence as they roll south and westwards. The
land changes quite dramatically in the north, where tall hills stand, close to
the mountains. The steep, naked side of one of the hills shoots into the air
just north of here.
name: Grasslands below the Hills

desc:
Plains covered in green sod dominate the view, the sharp blades of grass
striking upwards like thousands upon thousands of tiny spears. Patches of
shrubbery have made this place home. An almost perfect circle of small white
stones lies neatly in the grass, though whether by chance or purpose it is
difficult to say. Some hills can be seen to the north that prevent travel.
name: Rolling Grassland

desc:
From small trickling rivulets the grass absorbs enough water to flourish on
these rolling fields. Up and down they roll in an apparently never-ending
fashion. Small thickets of stinging nettle occurs from time to time, and a few
flowers can be spotted. In the east the steep slopes of the Misty Mountains rise
up forming an impassable wall of cold hard rock and snow.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
Vast rolling fields of green grass surround you. Low bushes cling close to the
ground, shaped by the years of constant cold winds blowing down from the
towering mountain range that rises up towards the sky off in the east. Far to
the northeast a forerunner of the Misty Mountains can be seen, rising up from
the grasslands. To the south a small mountain stream makes its way northwest,
gaining width as it goes.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
Dry, brown grass and small thickets of thorn bushes are the only flora in these
plains. The ground is hard and dry, composed of red brown limestone and clay. To
the west lie plains and beyond them an ancient road. To the east is an
impassable cliff to the water, and to the south yet more plains.
name: Dry, Barren Fields

desc:
A small clear stream runs its course from the southeast to the north. Green
grass grows on the banks, as well as reeds and other vegetation. West of here an
ancient road can be seen, while barren folded fields extend south. On the
horizon to the east the white peaks of the Misty Mountains reach their jagged
fingers towards the sky.
name: Swirling Stream

desc:
The water follows the decline of the surrounding landscape twisting back and
forth, flowing northward into the lower parts of the valley. To the west, the
bank rises up against the barren plains, preventing passage in that direction.
Looking south, the river gives way to a dense swamp.
name: Narrow Twist of a Stream

desc:
The cold mountain water makes a sharp turn west along a steeply undermined bank
that has been hollowed out over the course of many years. Any travel to the
north is prevented by that undercutting. South and west wide, large wet
swamplands spread out, and to the east the foothills of the Misty Mountains rise
slowly.
name: Stream at a High Bank

desc:
A small stream runs its course from the southeast to the northwest. The eastern
bank has been hollowed out over time, making travel in that direction
impossible. Near the stream green lush grass grows in abundance, while further
south barren dry grassland stretches out as far as the eye can see. To the east
a forerunner of the Misty Mountains rises up from the rolling fields.
name: Bend in a Stream

desc:
A strong smell of decomposition fills this cavern. The origin of the smell is
probably the large pile of garbage lying in one corner. It has been left
unattended over time and none can tell of what it is comprised. Small patches of
moss grow upon the walls, and lichen is suspended from the ceiling. The cavern
opens up to the south.
name: Smelly Cavern

desc:
A small cavern has been dug out here by crude hands. The area seems mainly to be
used as a sleeping room for the inhabitants of the cave system. Around the
centre of the room can be seen a number of huge beds made of dried grass. The
earthen walls are moist, and composed of a red limestone.
name: Troll Lair

desc:
A moist cavern extends under the spurs of the Misty Mountains. It has been
enlarged by crude hands not caring if the walls are rough or smooth. Small
rivulets of water dripping down the walls gather in the shallows of the ground
forming small bodies of water. The still air smells of decomposition. No natural
light source can be found.
name: Damp Cavern

desc:
This ground in this part of the cave is churned up mud, both a mixture of water
leaking out from under the heavy door to the north, and acrid troll urine from
the nests that seem to have been made for the very youngest of the troll's
young. Disgusting piles of uncured hides, bones and even humanoid scalps plus
grass and leaves make up these nests.
name: Muddy Den

desc:
This damp room is where the trolls keep their prisoners, or future meals, for
they are really one and the same. Cold water drips from the roof, and flows in
tiny rivulets down the wall, offering the only liquid these unfortunate wretches
get before their terrible demise. A dark, heavy door to the south serves to keep
the prisoners contained within this dank, dismal place.
name: Damp Prison Cell

desc:
The cavern is dark and a fine dust lingers in the air. Portions of every wall
appear to be crumbling and starting to collapse, other than to the west. To the
west a slab of large and sharp slate was used to attempt to block the cave off
from the tunnel beyond or to keep something or someone within.
name: Dark Cavern
